author: @anysailer
text: Dear Robert, these walk about conversations are brilliant.  I thoroughly enjoyed this one.  It reminded me of when I was running a manufacturing engineering company several years ago.  I was often involved in developing innovative products for our clients.  The concept of 'good enough' always played a significant role in during the design and development process.  What I experienced was that the two key criteria which influenced the distinction between 'good enough' and perfection were cost and time.  There was always a compromise, and the 'best' product was in fact always that iteration of a product arrived at through a developmental process (prototyping),  that was good enough, safe and reasonably durable (what can best be described as 'fit for purpose'), that could be easily replicated at a reasonable cost and within a reasonable time frame.  Your ideas in this conversation beautifully and accurately illustrate the philosophical basis of a very important aspect of modern industry and something that touches our lives in so many ways every single day, namely quality.  It is these 'good enough' products and not the 'perfect' products, that eventually define and set standards for similar products, and any product that reaches and especially those that exceed these standards are considered quality products.  Thank so much and keep up the excellent work.

author: @iancrosby3475
text: Mate, honestly I watch your videos and see you make things and explain principles I remember from school and the lightbulb just goes on in a way it didn't in a dry classroom with a dusty master and a dustier chalkboard and then there's your vids where you just talk plain old common sense and put idiots in their place without being harsh, without raising your voice or being disrespectful.  And then I watch this one and from the title I wasn't sure which it would be and it's straight down the line between the two.  And as someone that struggles with never getting stuff done because I can always see a 'flaw' there's always a 'could have done better' which honestly sometimes drives me but other times crushes me, I guess what I'm say is thank you rob a lightbulb just went on so I can pull my socks up and crack on.   Thank you for another awesome inspiring video and on behalf of his  viewers thank you as well for inflicting  Luke upon an unsuspecting public.  He  has more energy than any number of steel weights wound up a pulley inside lampposts.  Thanks.

author: @paulbrouyere1735
text:
I loved this video, It was like a revival of my youth, when I was flying model RC airplanes and the sky was the limit. We burnt stuff, tested things out, all right, sometimes a bit over the top.
That is why I really admire your series about wind energy. There is wind before, and there is wind after, and we can all keep breathing that wind. That's the large difference between ecological thinking and just burning things because it seems to go quicker. What are we doing? Shouldn't we be constructing our world at a pace where we can all breathe, rest and enjoy? Wasn't the rat race a result of war and destruction, and developed systems like nuclear and combustibles only there to overcome an enemy? Let's not be our own enemy. The best is yet to come. And there, sir, you are one of the best to explain it to most of us. Cheers!

author: @Ian8008
text: Well - good video and you make some excellent points.  I don't quite like the terms though - "Good enough" has the hint of sloppy connected to it.  I'd say a better word is "workable" - or as you did say - it gets the job done.  Here you bring in the element of viability, the economics of it.  Is it practical etc.  Perfection is NOT the same as this - perfection is flawless, cannot be further improved etc.  Perfection as you noted on the jet engine, is not needed for a workable solution.  Perfection may well be nonviable - it often is - hence not practical and more an Art concept than real engineering.

author: @jsincoherency
text: I think you might be off by a factor of 1000. 14kg * 10m/s^2 * 5m = 700 Joules, but 195 Wh = ~700,000 Joules. Just specify a 14-ton weight and I think it'll do the job. Might need a lot of wind to lift it that high in a day though!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: you are spot on mate - that's what i get for off the cuff maths - i should know better but apparently i don't lol - I haven't changed it because it wasn't central to the point of the video but thanks for pointing it out

replies.author: @jsincoherency
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering I thought it would be cool to make a bath that is heated by its own weight. So you'd fill up the bath in the loft, then allow it to descend to the ground floor, capturing the energy released on the way down to heat the water. Then you drain the bath on the ground floor so lifting it back up takes much less energy. I worked out that a drop of 8 metres would be enough to raise the temperature by about 20 deg. C and thought that was very close to viable... only to realise I was off by a factor of 1000 and I needed to drop my bath through 8 kilometres!

author: @ericblenner-hassett3945
text: The part where you talked about the street lamp reminded me of two things.  There is a clockwork based crank radio and a weight based light for third world countries.  They both use a form of clockwork to move a generator fast enough for power, with capacitors to help hold it within tolerance and can be easily ' recharged ' by one person in a few seconds.  The radio has a spring, the light has a gear allowing the mass to be pulled up faster than the release rate.  I am not saying it could be ' perpetual motion ' just more Rube Goldberg to have one reset the other and enjoy the radio with a light on.  Cheese and tea not included.

author: @totherarf
text:
Just like infinities, there are different Perfections!

When we are talking about a device being "Perfect" it is the best we can do in several competing areas! Perfect weld .... No! ... but it doesn't need to be perfect, it needs to join two bits of metal together without weakening over time And be able to be done by a reasonably trained person. A perfect weld on my car's exhaust manifold needs more of the cheap and not fall appart then looking good, a SpaceX engine manifold on the other hand wants it to be outstanding on quality with cost coming a poor second!
So the real perfect is an amalgam of several things (often dependent on who is viewing it)! ...... a bit like TnT ;o)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: very good mate

author: @saiello2061
text: The reason why the Japanese were so successful with their motorcycles in the late 70's and flood the market was a new way to look at tolerance. Reduced cost and manufacturing time was achieved by relaxing the tolerances on moving engine parts, particularly on the machining of the cranks/big end bearings. Uniquely, they would then simply perform a mix-and-match operation as part of their manufacturing process, mating up the loose toleranced parts with the tight toleranced parts. Genius.

author: @woolymittens
text: Perfection is engineering an item that exactly fulfills the requirements of that item, within cost targets. If a "widget" is required to perform a given function for 8 hours and it performs that function perfectly for 8 hours then fails after 8 hours and 1 second .. so long as its fabrication fell within cost targets.. it is perfect.

author: @zapaholic
text:
A modern day lamplighter.
An indicator on the post lets you know the position of the weight and if it indicates there wasn't enough wind to raise the weight sufficiently then an attached crank allows anyone to raise the weight manually.  If the light is in front of your house then you crank it up and try to get the neighbors who benefit from the light to also crank it up every once in a while... or better yet, give the assignment your kids!😄

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i like that mate

replies.author: @jameszietsman5518
replies.text: The kids get the necessary physical exercise! 😊

replies.author: @zapaholic
replies.text:
​@jameszietsman5518 I got the idea to build a pedal generator 50 years ago or so after reading an article in Popular Mechanics or Popular Science about a guy who built a generator, hooked it to a bike, built an inverter(!!!), attached  a battery, then told his kids they could watch as many cartoons as the battery would allow.  The article said the kids would pedal and charge the battery up Friday night so they could watch cartoons without having to pedal Saturday morning. 
40 years later I finally got one built but my love of cartoons was long gone by then. 😅

author: @krissfemmpaws1029
text:
Very well said, many designers try to come up with the perfect design and lose sight of the reality of what's actually needed. 

I play at designing light homebuilt aircraft and many times I've been caught up in the trap of getting the performance perfect. I lost sight of the fact the plane just needs to fly well and have good to very good flight characteristics with relatively low horsepower.

author: @markbothum4338
text: Retired engineer here. We never sought perfection, we sought to be first to market with a functional product. Then _immediately_, the junior engineers would be tasked with fine tuning and, perhaps more importantly, cost reduction in both material and manufacturing processes.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: really important stuff that so often gets ignored

replies.author: @woolymittens
replies.text: Perfection is engineering an item that exactly fulfills the requirements of that item, within cost targets. If a "widget" is required to perform a given function for 8 hours and it performs that function perfectly for 8 hours then fails after 8 hours and 1 second .. if its fabrication fell within cost targets.. it is perfect.

text: Well said. The world of computers has influenced us. We have become used to great images etc in our hands on on our desks, but,  computers also aren't perfect. They rely upon the human interface to educate them and we are flawed. Rubbish in, rubbish out. We still need to think things out for ourselves and while we can strive for perfection , we will never attain it. We are a race which never stops learning, we are driven to learn, if we attain perfection, what else is left?

author: @dereksollows9783
text: Having worked on construction sites and in manufacturing until my retirement - your comments struck a note for me. The best inspectors were usually the ones who had real-world experience because that experience usually conferred 'real-world' expectations.  In so many scenarios the freshly minted engineer is delegated to perform inspections, often by bosses in gleeful anticipation of watching the newbee furthering their education. Sometimes this worked out, while at other times serious confrontations occurred.

author: @sshutupurface8345
text: 14kg x 5m x 9.8ms =  686 joules  so 686 / 3600= 0.190wh ??? you'll need 14 tons

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: oh curses - i went the other way round - figuring 195 watt hours was 702,000 watt seconds and that meant 702,000 joules - over 5 meters - about 140,000 newtons roughly 10 Newtons per kg and i got 14kg when i should have gotten 14,000 kg curses, curses curses - poor error by me mate - thank you for correcting me - that makes it undoable then - ah well it was a nice idea

replies.author: @sshutupurface8345
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering whatever you do don't trust chatgpt its awful at math, the formulas are right but its terrible at calculating them

replies.author: @sshutupurface8345
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering I thought about this using tides in a harbour and a oil tanker ship docked,   they are apparently 550 000 dwt (dead weight tonnage)  and  many tides go up and down  1 one meter if they could be attached to  huge weight storage that means 1497kw per rise and fall of tide  that's 5.39 billion joules each ship could power 100 homes at 30 kwh a day with 2 tides so 3000kwh @ nz$ 0.3 per kwh thats only nz$900 a day or 300k a year

author: @madmaveric
text:
Well said 👍

Engineer, good enough is good enough.  
Scientist, perfection isn't good enough.

The question is often are you an Engineer or scientist?

Both think the same way but engineering is about reaching good enough. Science is about finding the perfect answer and then carrying on questioning your answer as in case you are missing something :D
Most people always want to improve things more, the 'good enough' argument was drilled into me at Uni (computer science) as most companies want something that works but as cheap as possible (least amount of man hours invested). I often found it hard to stop when coding (faster, smaller) even though it worked 'good enough' for the job that was needed.

'Good Enough' is a skill you have to learn just like any other.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yes it is mate and very much a difficult skill to learn

replies.author: @Ian8008
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Well I agree with most of what you say.  I didn't follow the "Scientist, perfection isn't good enough" part.  Isn't science the study of data to formulate the underlying laws of nature.  Gravity, E= MC2, speed of light and such?  Where does perfection come into it?  What you describe is two different goals - the company wanting a workable system at a good price and an individual trying to get the smallest fastest code he can write - aiming indeed for perfection.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: @Ian8008 I didn't say that mate MM did - you might want to reply to him rather than me. What I am talking about is there is a confusion in people between good enough and perfection - for lots if it isn't perfect it isn't good enough - i disagree with that

author: @MRTIMEARLE
text: You are amazing!  I just discovered your remarkable channel!  I need help getting off the grid fast.  How can I make electricity the best way?  I’m thinking of making a few hundred of the new wind generator that you’ve been working with recently.  Is that a possible idea?

replies.author: @stl7694
replies.text:
oh no.  slow down friend.  there is an awful lot to consider.  how much power do you need?  why do you need to get off grid?  what is your production capacity?   for some people it might be cheaper and easier to buy a ready made gas powered generator (you could even power it with alternative fuels), plus it will have all the electronics for power conversion and regulation,  you can always take off the motor later and spin it another way.
Trying to make hundreds of wind blades, buy multiple hundreds of magnets and thousands of feet of wire and assemble it all is a daunting task and would take a great deal of time.
It is great to experiment, learn to build generators from scrap and have fun but if you don't have time and resources trying to create substantial generation is difficult.
I am not trying to dissuade you I just want you to consider other options.

replies.author: @Dionysios_Skoularikis
replies.text: How much money are you ready to spend for that? I am off grid since 2016.

replies.author: @In.Darkness
replies.text:
How much time you got buddy ? 🤣 

You came to the right channel 👍
Cheers

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: you are not asking the question you mean to ask mate - fast is easy it's just expensive - what is best - well that depends on you and your circumstances

replies.author: @noldushumlesnurr6169
replies.text: Start small. Take your computer, phone, network off grid. What local resources do you have? Sun, wind, wood? I started with a 160Wp pv panel, cheap regulator, a 12v battery and a 300w modified sin inverter. Got some experience. Made some failures and a few good choices. Now I can go off grid if need be, but comfort makes it necessary to stay on the grid during the winter months  Nov-jan. Start small and take piece by piece in your life off the grid. It is as much about habits as it is about energy.
